industry.name: Additive Manufacturing
industry.description: The full career landscape of 3D printing and advanced manufacturing — from machine operators on the shop floor to materials scientists and business leaders driving adoption across aerospace, defense, and medical industries.

roles.title: Materials Lab Technician
roles.description: Prepares and tests raw material samples — primarily metal powders and polymer feedstocks — to support process development and quality control. Operates basic lab equipment, logs results, and maintains material traceability records. Entry point into the materials science career track.

roles.title: Materials Characterization Technician
roles.description: Characterizes powder and printed part properties using analytical instruments including SEM, XRD, and particle size analyzers. Documents material property data and supports the engineering team in identifying process-material relationships. Often transitions into materials process roles after 2–3 years.

roles.title: Materials Process Technician
roles.description: Executes material qualification experiments and manages the powder lifecycle — tracking usage, recycling, and degradation across production builds. Bridges the gap between lab characterization and machine operations, ensuring consistent material properties reach the printer. A pivotal mid-level role with paths into both materials engineering and process engineering.

roles.title: Materials Scientist
roles.description: Conducts independent research on process-structure-property relationships in additively manufactured metals and polymers. Develops material specifications, collaborates with equipment engineers to tune process parameters, and produces technical reports for internal stakeholders and customers. Often the subject matter expert consulted for customer qualifications and government contracts.

roles.title: Senior Materials Engineer
roles.description: Leads materials qualification programs for flight-critical and defense AM components, navigating ASTM, AMS, and customer-specific standards. Interfaces directly with OEM customers and government stakeholders to establish material acceptance criteria. One of the most in-demand roles in the AM supply chain given the push to qualify printed parts for aerospace and DoD applications.

roles.title: Materials R&D Lead
roles.description: Owns the materials research roadmap for the organization — setting priorities, managing a team of engineers and scientists, and securing internal and external funding. Typically holds an MS or PhD and brings domain expertise in metal AM or polymer AM. A key hire for companies scaling into high-consequence markets like aerospace, medical, or defense.

roles.title: Research Scientist
roles.description: A senior individual contributor research role, typically PhD-level, focused on advancing the fundamental understanding of AM processes and materials. Often the technical face of the company in academic and national lab partnerships. Publishes research, represents the company at conferences, and translates findings into actionable process improvements.

roles.title: AM Research Engineer
roles.description: Designs and executes structured experiments to understand how process parameters affect material and part properties. Comfortable moving between the lab bench and the machine floor, collecting data that feeds directly into process development decisions. A strong launchpad for careers in either materials engineering or process engineering.

roles.title: Powder Metallurgy Technician
roles.description: Specializes in the handling, classification, and lifecycle management of metal powders used in laser powder bed fusion and directed energy deposition processes. Works closely with process engineers to ensure powder quality meets build specifications and maintains detailed chain-of-custody records critical for aerospace and defense qualification.

roles.title: Process Research Engineer
roles.description: Bridges materials science and machine operations by developing and validating process parameters that produce consistent, high-quality parts. Deep expertise in one or more AM processes (typically LPBF, DED, or Binder Jetting). Outputs are the process specifications that machine operators follow and that quality teams validate against.

roles.title: CAD Drafter
roles.description: Creates and maintains 2D drawings and 3D CAD models to engineering specifications, with a growing focus on AM-specific file preparation (STL/3MF export, build orientation basics). Typically the first design role in an AM engineering team and a direct pathway into design engineering with further education or experience.

roles.title: AM Design Technician
roles.description: Prepares CAD models for AM production — orienting parts, generating support structures, and nesting builds for efficiency using tools like Magics or Netfabb. Works directly with process engineers to optimize file setups for target machines and materials. A technically hands-on entry role with a clear path into design and simulation engineering.

roles.title: AM Design Engineer
roles.description: Designs parts specifically for additive manufacturing, applying DfAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing) principles like topology optimization, part consolidation, and internal channel design that are impossible with subtractive methods. Works closely with customers or product teams to re-engineer existing parts for AM and reduce part count and weight.

roles.title: Simulation Engineer
roles.description: Uses computational tools to simulate the AM build process and predict thermal distortion, residual stress, and structural behavior of printed parts. Reduces costly trial-and-error builds by validating designs virtually before committing machine time. Increasingly important as companies qualify parts for aerospace and medical applications where dimensional accuracy is critical.

roles.title: Senior AM Design Engineer
roles.description: Leads complex AM design projects from concept to qualification, coordinating between materials, process, and quality teams. Owns the design intent and ensures DfAM principles are applied throughout. A key technical contributor to customer engagements and one of the most frequently posted senior roles in AM companies.

roles.title: Topology Optimization Engineer
roles.description: Specializes in computationally-driven structural optimization, producing lightweight designs that achieve maximum performance per unit weight — a critical capability for aerospace and defense applications. Works at the intersection of structural analysis and AM design, often supporting high-value DoD or commercial aerospace programs.

roles.title: Principal Design Engineer
roles.description: The highest individual-contributor design role — owns the technical direction of the design engineering practice, mentors senior engineers, and represents design in executive-level engagements. Typically the organization's deepest expert in DfAM and design validation methodology, often with 10+ years of AM-specific experience.

roles.title: DfAM Specialist
roles.description: A specialized role — equal parts engineer and educator — that helps customers and internal teams understand and apply DfAM principles. Often found at AM machine OEMs, service bureaus, and companies with an AM Center of Excellence. Produces design guidelines, delivers training workshops, and supports design reviews to ensure AM advantages are fully realized.

roles.title: Mechanical Engineer (AM)
roles.description: A broadly-skilled mechanical engineer who applies conventional design and analysis skills within an AM context — often the first engineering hire at a smaller AM company. Handles design, analysis, drawing release, and test planning, with responsibilities expanding as AM knowledge grows.

roles.title: Engineering Manager (AM)
roles.description: Manages the design and engineering team, balancing technical quality with program delivery timelines. Typically a technical expert who has transitioned into people leadership. Responsible for hiring, developing, and retaining engineering talent while maintaining hands-on involvement in key technical decisions.

roles.title: AM Machine Operator
roles.description: Operates additive manufacturing machines on the production floor — loading materials, starting builds, monitoring processes, and unloading completed parts. The entry point for the machine operations track and one of the most-posted roles in the AM industry as production volumes grow. Strong advancement potential with demonstrated reliability and curiosity.

roles.title: 3D Printing Technician
roles.description: Operates polymer or composite AM systems (FDM, SLA, SLS, MJF) in a production or service bureau environment. Preps files, sets up builds, and performs basic quality checks on completed parts. A common starting role at service bureaus and prototyping shops, with a clear path into process technician roles.

roles.title: Senior Machine Operator
roles.description: Leads the day-to-day operation of multiple AM machines, troubleshoots process alarms, and trains new operators. Typically the most experienced person on the machine floor in a shift, with significant institutional knowledge about machine behavior and common failure modes. A natural step toward process technician or operations lead roles.

roles.title: Process Technician
roles.description: Owns the technical execution of AM builds — selecting and adjusting process parameters, managing machine setups, and investigating build failures. Works between machine operators and process engineers, combining hands-on machine expertise with technical problem-solving. One of the highest-leverage mid-level roles for companies scaling production.

roles.title: Build Engineer
roles.description: Designs the build strategy for production parts — determining orientation, nesting, supports, and machine parameters to maximize yield and quality. Serves as a critical link between design engineers and the machine floor, translating design intent into actionable production setups. Common at aerospace and defense-focused AM service bureaus.

roles.title: Process Engineer
roles.description: Develops, validates, and owns AM process specifications for production — the engineering authority on how machines should run for any given material-part combination. Interfaces with customers, materials teams, and quality to ensure processes are robust and documented. One of the most critical senior hires for a company moving from prototype to production.

roles.title: Senior Process Engineer
roles.description: Leads process qualification programs for new materials and machine configurations, often in regulated industries (aerospace, medical). Authors the qualification test plans and process specifications that form the backbone of a company's quality system. Strong candidates for engineering management or operations lead roles.

roles.title: AM Operations Lead
roles.description: Owns the day-to-day operational performance of the AM production floor — managing throughput, quality, and cost metrics. Leads a team of operators, technicians, and process engineers, and collaborates with business development to translate customer demand into production capacity. The highest operations-track role below general management.

roles.title: Production Manager (AM)
roles.description: Accountable for the full production operation — people, machines, throughput, quality, and delivery. Balances tactical daily execution with strategic capacity planning. A natural transition role from AM Operations Lead for those who want broader business responsibility.

roles.title: Machine Maintenance Technician
roles.description: Performs preventive and corrective maintenance on AM machines — a technically demanding entry role that sits at the intersection of mechanical, electrical, and laser systems knowledge. High demand as AM machine fleets grow at production facilities. A gateway into senior equipment engineering roles.

roles.title: Post-Processing Technician
roles.description: Removes supports, performs surface finishing, and prepares printed parts for inspection or delivery. A physically hands-on role that builds deep familiarity with part geometry, common defects, and finishing quality expectations. Many quality engineers and NDT technicians begin their careers here.

roles.title: Heat Treatment Technician
roles.description: Operates thermal treatment furnaces to stress-relieve, anneal, or harden AM parts after printing. Critical for metal AM where residual stress from the build process must be managed before machining or inspection. Often the entry point into metallurgical and materials engineering career tracks.

roles.title: CMM Operator
roles.description: Programs and operates coordinate measuring machines to verify dimensional compliance of printed and post-processed parts against engineering drawings. A precision role requiring strong GD&T fluency and attention to detail. AS9100-regulated facilities consider CMM operators critical to first article and production inspection.

roles.title: Quality Technician
roles.description: Performs in-process and final inspections, documents nonconformances, and supports corrective action processes. The day-to-day guardian of quality on the production floor, working closely with operators, process engineers, and CMM operators to maintain product conformance and traceability.

roles.title: Metrology Specialist
roles.description: Owns the dimensional measurement strategy for complex AM parts, deploying advanced techniques including computed tomography (CT) and structured light scanning to inspect internal features that conventional inspection cannot reach. Manages the calibration program and supports customer qualification activities where dimensional verification is a contract deliverable.

roles.title: Quality Engineer
roles.description: Owns quality system processes end-to-end — from incoming material through final inspection and delivery. Leads internal audits, manages supplier quality programs, and drives corrective actions when nonconformances arise. Often the primary point of contact for customer and regulatory quality audits. A gateway to quality management leadership.

roles.title: NDT Technician (Level II)
certifications: ASNT Level II (PT, RT, or UT)
roles.description: Performs certified nondestructive testing of AM parts to detect surface and subsurface defects without damaging the part — essential for flight-critical and pressure-bearing components. ASNT Level II certification is the industry standard for this role, and certified NDT technicians are in high demand as aerospace and defense AM volumes grow.

roles.title: Senior Quality Engineer
roles.description: Leads quality across complex, multi-program environments — managing the quality system, supporting source inspection activities, and driving SPC adoption across production. Increasingly the role that interfaces with DCMA (Defense Contract Management Agency) for DoD-related AM programs. A strong pathway to quality management.

roles.title: Quality Systems Manager
roles.description: Owns the entire quality management system, maintains AS9100 certification, and serves as the primary quality interface with customers, Tier 1 suppliers, and regulatory bodies. Leads a team of quality engineers and technicians and drives a culture of continuous improvement. A critical hire for any AM company seeking to win aerospace or defense contracts.

roles.title: Nondestructive Testing Engineer
skills: ASNT Level III (one or more methods)
roles.description: The engineering authority on nondestructive evaluation — develops NDT procedures, qualifies new inspection methods for AM materials and geometries, and trains and certifies NDT technicians. X-ray CT is an emerging and increasingly important method given the complex internal geometries of AM parts. ASNT Level III certification required.

roles.title: AM Applications Technician
roles.description: Supports the applications and sales team by producing sample parts, conducting demonstrations, and assisting with customer qualification builds. A customer-facing technical role that builds broad AM process knowledge quickly. Common at machine OEMs and service bureaus, with a clear path into applications engineering.

roles.title: Technical Sales Representative
roles.description: Generates and qualifies sales leads for AM machines, materials, software, or services, translating technical capabilities into customer value. Works closely with applications engineers and senior sales engineers on larger deals. A great entry into the commercial side of AM, with significant earning potential as experience builds.

roles.title: Applications Engineer
roles.description: Works directly with customers to identify high-value AM applications, develop business cases, and guide part qualification journeys. Bridges commercial and technical roles — needs enough engineering depth to earn customer trust and enough commercial awareness to close deals. One of the highest-posted technical sales roles in the AM industry.

roles.title: Customer Success Engineer
roles.description: Ensures customers are getting full value from their AM investment — providing post-sale technical support, training, and troubleshooting. Particularly important in the machine OEM sector where successful deployments drive renewals, consumable revenue, and referrals. A strong path into senior applications or business development roles.

roles.title: Senior Applications Engineer
roles.description: Owns the most complex and strategic customer application programs, guiding large OEMs and defense primes through multi-year part qualification journeys. Acts as both technical advisor and trusted commercial partner, often influencing platform-level decisions for customers deploying AM at scale. A natural springboard to Solutions Architect or Business Development roles.

roles.title: Sales Engineer
roles.description: Drives revenue by selling AM machines, services, or materials to complex technical buyers — typically in aerospace, automotive, medical, or defense. Combines deep technical credibility with commercial acumen. Total compensation frequently exceeds base salary significantly through commissions tied to capital equipment or contract wins.

roles.title: Program Manager (AM)
roles.description: Owns end-to-end delivery of complex AM programs — typically multi-year contracts with aerospace OEMs, defense primes, or government agencies. Manages scope, schedule, budget, and risk while keeping customers informed and satisfied. High demand as AM companies win more DoD and CHIPS Act-funded program work.

roles.title: Business Development Manager
roles.description: Identifies and develops new market opportunities, strategic partnerships, and government program relationships. Often the person representing the company at America Makes, AMUG, and RAPID events. Increasingly involves navigating CHIPS Act, DoD modernization, and reshoring initiatives where AM plays a central role.

roles.title: AM Solutions Architect
roles.description: Designs comprehensive AM implementation solutions for large enterprise customers — from initial feasibility through digital integration and production scale-up. A relatively new role in the industry reflecting the maturation of AM from prototyping to full production workflows. Often found at machine OEMs, software companies, and large service bureaus.

roles.title: Director of AM Operations
roles.description: Executive-level accountability for the full AM business — operations, commercial, technical, and financial performance. Typically comes from a deep AM background combined with general management experience. The role most frequently created when a company's AM revenue reaches $20M+ and requires dedicated executive leadership.

pathways.name: Shop Floor to Process Engineering
pathways.description: Starting as a machine operator and building toward process engineering through hands-on machine expertise, parameter knowledge, and formal engineering credentials.

pathways.name: Materials Science Track
pathways.description: A research-heavy progression from lab technician to materials leadership, focused on developing deep expertise in AM feedstocks, process-structure-property relationships, and material qualification.

pathways.name: Design Engineering Track
pathways.description: From CAD drafting to principal design engineering, building expertise in design for additive manufacturing, topology optimization, and simulation-driven design.

pathways.name: Quality & Inspection Track
pathways.description: Building a career in quality systems and inspection — from post-processing and visual inspection to owning the quality management system for an AS9100-certified facility.

pathways.name: Applications & Commercial Track
pathways.description: The technical commercial path — from applications technician to senior applications, solutions architecture, and business development leadership.

pathways.name: R&D Research Track
pathways.description: A science-first path from characterization technician through research engineering to lead research scientist, focused on advancing fundamental AM knowledge.

pathways.name: Operations Management Track
pathways.description: Growing from machine operator to production and program management, building operational and leadership skills alongside technical expertise.

pathways.name: NDT & Advanced Inspection Track
pathways.description: Specializing in nondestructive testing — from Level II technician to Level III engineer, focused on the inspection of complex AM geometries for aerospace and defense.

pathways.name: Simulation & Advanced Design Track
pathways.description: Combining mechanical engineering fundamentals with AM-specific simulation tools to specialize in build process simulation, structural optimization, and DfAM consultancy.

pathways.name: Technical Sales Track
pathways.description: Building a commercial career in AM — from inside sales and customer success through senior sales engineering and business development management.

pathways.name: Powder & Process Track
pathways.description: Specializing in the critical area where materials science meets machine operations — powder handling, lifecycle management, and process parameter development.

pathways.name: Cross-functional Engineering Leadership
pathways.description: A path for senior engineers transitioning into leadership — combining deep process expertise with people management and program ownership.
